title: Chapter 15: Simple Phenomena of Magnetism — Hard

questions.question: A piece of wood is placed between the North pole of a magnet and two iron nails. It is observed that the nails are still attracted to the magnet. Why?
options: Wood is non-magnetic and does not block magnetic fields
The wood becomes an induced magnet and attracts the nails
The nails are permanent magnets themselves
The magnet is strong enough to turn wood into a magnetic material
questions.explanation:
As noted in Worked Example 15.1, wood is a non-magnetic material. It does not completely block the magnetic field, allowing the magnet to still induce magnetism in the nails behind it.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Two iron nails are held by their heads against the North pole of a bar magnet. It is observed that the pointed tips of the nails spread away from each other. What explains this?
options: The tips both become induced North poles and repel each other
The tips both become induced South poles and repel each other
One tip becomes a North pole and the other a South pole
The magnet's field pushes the nails apart mechanically
questions.explanation:
The heads (near the N pole) become induced South poles. The tips (farther ends) both become induced North poles. Since like poles repel, the two North-pole tips push away from each other.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: What happens if a permanent bar magnet is broken exactly in half across its middle?
options: Two smaller magnets are formed, each with a North and a South pole
One piece becomes a North-only magnet and the other a South-only magnet
Both pieces lose their magnetism completely
The pieces become unmagnetised magnetic materials
questions.explanation:
Breaking a magnet does not separate the poles; instead, the magnetic domains at the break align to form new poles, resulting in two complete magnets.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Three metal bars (X, Y, and Z) are tested. Bar X attracts bar Y. Bar Y repels bar Z. What can be concluded with certainty?
options: Bars Y and Z are permanent magnets
All three bars are permanent magnets
Bar X must be a permanent magnet
Bar Z is an unmagnetised magnetic material
questions.explanation:
Repulsion is the only sure test for a magnet. Since Y and Z repel each other, they must both be magnets. X attracts Y, but X could be either a magnet or just a magnetic material.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: In the process of magnetisation, what happens to the internal structure of a magnetic material?
options: The randomly oriented magnetic domains align in one direction
Individual atoms are physically moved to the ends of the bar
Non-magnetic atoms are converted into magnetic atoms
The material loses electrons to the inducing magnet
questions.explanation:
Magnetisation occurs when the magnetic domains, which are initially pointing in random directions, are forced to align in one direction by an external field.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: In a magnetic relay, an armature is attracted to a magnet when current flows through a coil. For the relay to work correctly and release the armature when the current stops, which material is best for the armature?
options: Soft iron
Steel
Brass
Copper
questions.explanation:
The armature must lose its induced magnetism immediately when the current stops so that it can be released. Soft iron (magnetically soft) is required for this. 

**Common mistake:** Choosing steel, which would remain magnetised and keep the armature stuck.

questions.question: An unmagnetised steel rod is placed inside a solenoid. A large direct current is passed through the solenoid and then switched off. What is the state of the steel rod?
options: It has become a permanent magnet.
It remains unmagnetised because the current was DC.
It was magnetised but lost all magnetism when the current stopped.
It has become a temporary magnet that only works inside the coil.
questions.explanation:
Steel is magnetically hard. Placing it in a solenoid with DC magnetises it, and it will retain that magnetism after the current is removed, becoming a permanent magnet. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking it loses magnetism like the soft iron core of an electromagnet.

questions.question: Identify the result of replacing the soft iron core in an electric bell with a steel core.
options: The bell would ring once and the hammer would stay stuck to the core.
The bell would ring much louder because steel is a stronger magnet.
The bell would not ring at all because steel cannot be magnetised.
The bell would function normally but the battery would drain faster.
questions.explanation:
Because steel is a permanent magnet material, once it is magnetised by the first pulse of current, it will stay magnetised and keep the hammer attracted, preventing the 'make-and-break' action. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the bell wouldn't ring at all.

questions.question: Three identical solenoids have cores made of Brass, Soft Iron, and Steel. Which core will produce the strongest magnetic field when the current is ON, and which will remain the strongest magnet when the current is OFF?
options: ON: Soft Iron; OFF: Steel
ON: Steel; OFF: Soft Iron
ON: Brass; OFF: Steel
ON: Soft Iron; OFF: Brass
questions.explanation:
Soft iron is easiest to magnetise and produces a strong field while current is on. Steel is magnetically hard and retains the most magnetism when current is off. Brass is non-magnetic. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking steel produces the strongest field while the current is on.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why steel is used for the 'magnets' in Maglev trains?
options: It is magnetically hard and can provide a constant, permanent magnetic field.
It is magnetically soft and allows the train to change poles quickly.
It is lighter than iron, allowing the train to levitate more easily.
It is a better electrical insulator than iron.
questions.explanation:
According to the notes, permanent magnets are used in Maglev trains. Steel is magnetically hard and retains its magnetism, providing the necessary permanent field. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking Maglevs only use temporary electromagnets.

questions.question: What is observed when a plotting compass is moved in a circular path around a bar magnet?
options: The needle continuously changes direction to remain tangent to the field lines
The needle always points toward the North pole of the magnet
The needle always points toward the South pole of the magnet
The needle remains pointing in a fixed North-South direction regardless of position
questions.explanation:
The magnetic field direction changes at every point around the magnet; a compass needle will follow these changes as it aligns with the local field lines.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student finds a point near a bar magnet where a plotting compass does not point in a clear direction. What is the most likely reason?
options: The magnet's field is cancelled by the Earth's magnetic field at that point
The magnet has lost all of its magnetism
The compass is broken and the needle is stuck
The point is too close to the North pole
questions.explanation:
This describes a 'neutral point' where the magnetic field from the magnet is equal and opposite to the Earth's magnetic field, resulting in a zero resultant field.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the magnetic field pattern between two North poles placed close to each other?
options: The field lines bend away from each other, leaving a gap in the middle
The field lines move in straight lines from one North pole to the other
The field lines combine to form large circles around both poles
The field lines disappear entirely
questions.explanation:
When two North poles face each other, their field lines repel. This results in a pattern where lines from both poles bend away from the central region.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: In an experiment to map field lines with iron filings, why is the paper gently tapped after sprinkling the filings?
options: To overcome friction and allow filings to align with the magnetic field
To ensure the filings are evenly distributed across the paper
To magnetise the iron filings using the tapping force
To prevent the magnet from sticking to the paper
questions.explanation:
Tapping the paper provides the kinetic energy needed for the filings to overcome friction and move into alignment with the magnetic field lines.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: If a bar magnet is placed inside a soft iron ring, what happens to the magnetic field outside the ring?
options: The field strength outside is significantly reduced because the ring provides an easier path for the field lines
The field strength outside increases because iron is a magnetic material
The field lines pass through the iron ring without any change in direction
The North and South poles of the magnet are reversed
questions.explanation:
Soft iron is easily magnetised and has high permeability; it 'channels' the magnetic field lines within itself, effectively shielding the outside region.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.
